student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: Eliane Speaks — Fluency Showcase & Checkpoint Review
lesson.menu_desc: A checkpoint review that integrates every structure from Lessons 1-7 -- tenses, future, modals, conditionals, indirect questions and the passive -- in one fluency showcase -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Eliane Speaks: Fluency Showcase
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 8 -- Eliane Speaks: Fluency Showcase & Checkpoint Review

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a history of past performance and achievements
an important stage or event in a project
a person with an interest in a project or decision
the list of topics to cover in a meeting
the main point you want people to remember
a formal plan offered for consideration
following official rules, laws and standards
to lead a meeting and help the discussion run smoothly
general agreement among a group of people
a group who begin and go through a program together
a final project that brings the whole journey together

paras: Two years ago, Eliane was a gynecologist with a quiet voice in English. Since then, she has built something remarkable. She has moved from a doctor with a strong track record to a founder with real credentials, and she has learned to tell that story in the past, the present, and the future without stopping to translate. When people ask what she does, she no longer hesitates: she has been working in reproductive medicine for over a decade, and next year her own clinic will finally open its doors.

Along the way she has learned to hold her own in any professional room. She can facilitate a cross-functional meeting, keep every stakeholder aligned, and guide a group toward a real consensus before they adjourn. When a supplier sends a proposal, she reads the terms carefully; if the compliance details are clear, she negotiates with calm, indirect questions -- "Could you tell me how the warranty works?" -- and she softens her requests with modals: "Would you be able to adjust the delivery window?" She knows that if she asked bluntly, the deal would feel colder.

Her next milestone is executive education abroad. She has read the brochures, and she understands the language of them: applicants are evaluated on leadership, the curriculum is taught by faculty from several schools, and the final capstone is presented to the whole cohort. A founder and a physician like Eliane is exactly who admissions teams look for. The quiet voice is gone. What remains is a professional who can walk into any meeting, negotiation, or classroom in English and be heard.
reading.source: Adapted from a learner case study, The Executive Voice

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Eliane still avoids speaking English and prefers to translate every sentence first.
Over two years, Eliane has built a full professional voice in English -- she can tell her story across tenses, run meetings, negotiate, and describe programs, and she is ready for the next step.
Eliane has decided to close her clinic and study medicine abroad instead.

items: Eliane has been working in reproductive medicine for over a decade.
The text says she has been working in reproductive medicine for over a decade.
The text says Eliane still stops to translate every sentence before she speaks.
The text says she tells her story without stopping to translate, and the quiet voice is gone.
When a supplier's compliance details are clear, Eliane negotiates with polite, indirect questions and modals.
The text says if the compliance details are clear, she negotiates with indirect questions and softens requests with modals.
In the programs abroad, applicants are evaluated only on test scores.
The text says applicants are evaluated on leadership, not only scores.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words work together: you facilitate a meeting, follow the agenda, present your track record to each stakeholder, review a proposal, confirm compliance, and reach a consensus. This is the language of your whole journey in one paragraph.

bank.label: Your integration toolkit
items: I have been working in this field for over ten years.
Next year, my clinic will open its doors.
Could you tell me how the process works?
Would you be able to adjust the delivery window?
If we moved forward, we would sign this month.
Let me facilitate today and go through the agenda.
The curriculum is taught by faculty from several schools.
Are we all on the same page before we adjourn?

lf.title: Which structure from your journey is this?
items: If we moved forward, we would

lf.text: Each sentence uses a different structure you have already mastered: A = present perfect continuous (Lesson 1-2), B = future (Lesson 3), C = passive voice (Lesson 7), D = indirect question (Lesson 6), E = conditional (Lesson 5). Your whole toolkit in five lines.

items.situation: Someone asks about your experience. Answer using the present perfect continuous and your track record.
tips: I have been working in reproductive medicine for over ten years.
I have built a strong track record in fertility care.

items.situation: A partner asks about your plans. Answer using a future form.
tips: My clinic is going to open next year.
We will launch the new facility in 2027.

items.situation: You want a supplier's information, but politely. Ask an indirect question and soften it with a modal.
tips: Could you tell me how the warranty works?
Would you be able to send the proposal today?

items.situation: Describe how a program abroad works, using the passive voice.
tips: The curriculum is taught by faculty from several schools.
Applicants are evaluated on their leadership record.

items: In one minute, tell your professional story across time: where you have been (present perfect), where you are now, and where you will be next year. Use track record and credentials.
Open a short review meeting: facilitate, go through the agenda, invite a stakeholder to weigh in, and steer the group toward a consensus.
A supplier sends a proposal. Ask two polite, indirect questions about the compliance and the terms, then make a conditional offer to move forward.
Describe an executive program you would like to join, using the passive voice: how the curriculum is taught, how applicants are evaluated, and how the capstone is presented.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: Your story: 'I have been working in reproductive medicine for over ten years, I am opening my own clinic now, and next year we will double our capacity.'
Run the room: 'Let me facilitate today. First on the agenda... Would you like to weigh in? I think we have a consensus.'
Close the deal: 'Could you tell me how the warranty works? Do you know whether the compliance checks are complete? If we moved forward, we would sign this month.'
The program: 'The curriculum is taught by faculty from two schools, applicants are evaluated on leadership, and the capstone is presented to the whole cohort.'
answerkey.note: This is the whole journey: tenses, future, modals, conditionals, indirect questions and the passive, all in one conversation. You own every one of them now.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane, this is Robert again. I have watched a lot of professionals grow in English, and I want to tell you what I have noticed about you. Two years ago, you would translate every sentence in your head before you spoke. Now you tell your whole story without stopping. You have moved from a doctor with a strong track record to a founder with real credentials. In meetings, you facilitate, you keep every stakeholder aligned, and you reach a real consensus before you adjourn. When a supplier sends a proposal, you ask calm, indirect questions and you soften them with modals. And when you talk about programs abroad, you describe how the curriculum is taught and how applicants are evaluated, all in the passive, without thinking about it. The quiet voice is gone. Whatever room you walk into next, you will be heard.

Hi Eliane, it is Karen. I just wanted to say how proud I am of your progress. Do you remember our first call? You could barely finish a sentence about your work. Today you ran the whole review meeting: you went through the agenda, you invited every stakeholder to weigh in, and you guided us to a consensus. When the supplier proposal came up, you asked exactly the right questions -- how the compliance works, whether the terms could be adjusted -- and you never once sounded blunt. Your track record speaks for itself, but now your English does too. My advice is simple: keep speaking, keep taking the floor, and do not wait until it is perfect. Your voice is ready. Talk soon, and congratulations on the whole journey.
